title: Sorting strings by their last character

content.problemStatement:
In this task, you will learn to use Lambda functions in Python to sort a list of strings based on their last character. Lambda functions are anonymous functions that can have any number of arguments but can only have one expression. They are useful for creating small, one-time-use functions.

Your task is to complete the 'sort_by_last_char' function that takes a list of strings as input and returns a new list with the strings sorted based on their last character in ascending order. You must use a Lambda function within the sorted() function to achieve this.

For example, if the input is ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry', 'kiwi'], the output should be ['banana', 'apple', 'kiwi', 'cherry'].
constraints: 1 ≤ number of strings in a test case ≤ 1000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a space‑separated list of strings. Each string consists of 1 to 100 printable characters and contains no spaces.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the strings sorted in ascending order of their last character. Strings are printed in the same space‑separated format as the input.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Last characters: e, a, y, i. Sorted ascending gives a (banana), e (apple), i (kiwi), y (cherry).
Test case 1: last chars g, t, e → e (mouse), g (dog), t (cat). Test case 2: last chars a, t → a (zebra), t (ant).
Only one string, so the output is identical to the input.

hidden.label: maximum boundary
hidden.input: 1\nzebra alpha beta gamma delta epsilon theta iota kappa lambda mu nu xi omicron pi
hidden.expectedOutput: zebra alpha beta gamma delta theta iota kappa lambda xi pi epsilon omicron mu nu

solution.approach: For each test case we read the whole line, split it into individual strings and sort the resulting list using a key that is the last character of each string. In languages that support lambda/anonymous functions we pass a lambda to the sorting routine (e.g., sorted(..., key=lambda x: x[-1]) in Python, std::sort with a lambda in C++, Arrays.sort with a comparator in Java, etc.). The sorting algorithm of the standard library runs in O(N log N) time where N is the number of strings in the test case. The overall complexity is O(T·N log N) and the extra memory used is O(N) for the list of strings.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case, where N is the number of strings in that test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the strings of a test case.
